terms.definition: A sequential search algorithm that checks each element one by one from start to end until the target is found or the list is exhausted.
terms.example: Searching for 7 in [3, 1, 7, 9] checks 3, then 1, then 7 — found at index 2.

terms.definition: A data traversal pattern where elements are visited in order, one after another, without random jumping.
terms.example: Reading a linked list node by node is sequential access; jumping to arr[5] is random access.

terms.definition: The specific element being searched for within a collection.
terms.example: In linearSearch(arr, 9), the value 9 is the target.

terms.definition: A special value placed at a known position (often the end of an array) to guarantee a loop terminates without a separate bounds check.
terms.example: Placing target at arr[n-1] before the loop means the loop always hits the target, removing the i < n condition.

terms.definition: A variant of linear search that inserts the target as a sentinel at the last position, reducing per-iteration comparisons from two to one.
terms.example: Saves roughly half the comparisons per step compared to a naive loop with a bounds check.

terms.definition: The input arrangement that causes the algorithm to do the maximum work; for linear search this is when the target is at the last index or absent.
terms.example: Searching for 99 in [1, 2, 3, 99] is worse than searching for 1, because 99 is found last.

terms.definition: The input arrangement that causes the algorithm to do the minimum work; for linear search this is when the target is the very first element.
terms.example: Searching for 3 in [3, 7, 1, 9] returns immediately after one comparison.

terms.definition: The expected performance over all possible inputs assuming a uniform distribution; for linear search this is (n+1)/2 comparisons.
terms.example: On average, the target is found halfway through a randomly ordered array of n elements.

terms.definition: The integer position of an element within an array, starting at 0 in most languages.
terms.example: In arr = [5, 3, 8], value 8 is at index 2.

terms.definition: An array whose elements are in no particular order, making index-based shortcuts like binary search inapplicable.
terms.example: [7, 2, 5, 1, 9] is unsorted; linear search is the safe default.

terms.definition: A divide-and-conquer search that halves the search space at each step; requires the array to be sorted.
terms.example: On [1, 3, 5, 7, 9], binary search finds 7 in 2 comparisons versus 4 for linear search.

terms.definition: A problem-solving strategy that exhaustively tries every possibility without exploiting structure; linear search is the brute-force approach to searching.
terms.example: Checking every element is brute force; exploiting sortedness with binary search is not.

terms.definition: An optimization for repeated searches where each found element is moved to the head of the list, so frequently queried items are found faster over time.
terms.example: If 7 is searched 100 times in a list, moving it to the front after the first find makes all subsequent searches O(1).

terms.definition: A constant-space guarantee meaning the algorithm uses the same amount of extra memory regardless of input size.
terms.example: Linear search only needs one loop counter and one comparand — it never allocates memory proportional to n.

terms.definition: A potentially infinite or real-time sequence of values that can only be read once in order; random access is unavailable.
terms.example: Sensor readings arriving in real time form a stream — only linear scanning is possible.

terms.definition: A single instance of the target value appearing in the array; a value may have zero, one, or many occurrences.
terms.example: In [1, 3, 3, 7, 3], value 3 has three occurrences at indices 1, 2, and 4.

terms.definition: The ability to read any element in O(1) time by index, as arrays support; enables algorithms like binary search.
terms.example: arr[5] is random access; following five next pointers in a linked list is sequential access.

terms.definition: A generalized form of linear search that accepts a boolean function rather than a fixed target value, returning the first element satisfying the condition.
terms.example: Find first element greater than 5: linear_search_if(arr, lambda x: x > 5).
